articles.topic: exercise protecting inherited heart disease risk
articles.headline: Your Genes Loaded the Gun. Exercise May Have Just Unloaded It.
articles.body: If your family has a history of heart disease, here's something worth knowing. A new study tracked more than 15,000 people using wearable fitness data and genetic testing. Those who carried gene variants linked to cardiomyopathy — a condition that weakens the heart muscle — but still got regular moderate to vigorous exercise had significantly lower rates of heart attack and heart failure. Even hitting the standard 150-minute weekly guideline made a difference. The researchers say staying active may be especially critical for people who don't yet show any signs of disease. That pre-disease window is where lifestyle changes do the most.
articles.takeaway: If you have a family history of heart conditions, regular moderate exercise may be one of the most important tools you have — even before symptoms appear.
articles.source: American Journal of Preventive Cardiology / Medical Xpress

articles.topic: new fat-burning pill muscle metabolism no appetite suppression
articles.headline: Scientists Built a Fat-Burning Pill That Doesn't Touch Your Appetite. Here's How It Works.
articles.body: Researchers at Karolinska Institutet and Stockholm University just published findings on an experimental pill that takes a completely different approach to fat loss. Instead of suppressing your hunger like Ozempic does, it activates metabolism directly inside your skeletal muscle. In animal studies it lowered blood sugar, increased fat burning, and preserved muscle mass. A Phase I trial with 73 human participants found it was well tolerated. It's early — Phase II trials are next — but the concept matters. One of the biggest complaints about GLP-1 drugs is the muscle loss. This approach sidesteps that problem entirely.
articles.takeaway: This is still experimental, but it signals a shift in thinking: future fat-loss treatments may work with your muscles rather than against your appetite.

articles.topic: methionine amino acid inflammation survival kidney function
articles.headline: A Common Amino Acid Found in Your Food Helped Animals Survive Deadly Inflammation. Scientists Want to Know If It Works in Humans.
articles.body: Most people think of amino acids as building blocks for muscle. Salk Institute scientists just found something much bigger. When they added methionine — an amino acid naturally found in eggs, meat, fish, and dairy — to the diets of infected mice, survival rates improved dramatically. The mechanism was surprising: methionine boosted kidney filtration, helping clear dangerous inflammatory molecules from the bloodstream before they could damage the brain, tissues, and organs. The findings were published in Cell Metabolism. Researchers say this could open doors for patients with inflammatory conditions and kidney disease. Human trials are the obvious next step.
articles.takeaway: You don't need to supplement methionine — most people eating adequate protein already get enough. But this research reframes how diet and kidney function interact during illness.

articles.topic: strength training weekly dose longevity mortality risk
articles.headline: Two Hours of Lifting Per Week. That's the Number Science Just Landed On.
articles.body: A new study found that 90 to 120 minutes of strength training per week — about two solid sessions — was linked to lower risk of dying from cardiovascular and neurological diseases, as well as from any cause overall. That is not a massive time commitment. It is roughly one hour on Monday, one hour on Thursday, done. The findings build on a pile of evidence showing resistance training does far more than build muscle. It protects your heart, your brain, and your long-term function. The key detail: the benefits appeared to plateau around the two-hour mark, so more is not always better when it comes to lifting.
articles.takeaway: Two strength sessions per week is enough. Schedule them like appointments. The consistency matters far more than the volume.

workout.title: Sunday Unwind: Floor-Based Full-Body Release
workout.tip: Move through every hold slowly and breathe into the tight spots. If a position feels sharp rather than stretchy, ease back. Tension should melt, not spike.
workout.warmup: Lie flat on your back with knees bent, feet on the floor. Take 10 slow diaphragmatic breaths, letting your belly rise on the inhale and fall on the exhale. Then gently hug both knees into your chest and rock side to side for 30 seconds to warm up the lower back.

exercises.name: Supine Knee-to-Chest Hold
exercises.notes: Pull one knee gently toward your chest while keeping the other leg long on the floor. Keep your lower back pressed down. Switch sides slowly.

exercises.name: Figure-Four Hip Stretch
exercises.notes: Lying on your back, cross one ankle over the opposite knee and gently pull the lower leg toward your chest. Flex the top foot to protect the knee joint.

exercises.name: Child's Pose with Side Reach
exercises.notes: From hands and knees, sit back toward your heels and extend arms forward. Then walk both hands to one side to open the lat and oblique. Breathe deeply.

exercises.name: Thread the Needle Thoracic Rotation
exercises.notes: On all fours, slide one arm under your body along the floor, rotating your upper back. Let your shoulder rest on the mat. No forcing — let gravity do the work.

exercises.name: Legs Up the Wall
exercises.notes: Scoot your hips close to a wall and rest your legs vertically against it. Arms relaxed at your sides. This passively drains legs and calms the nervous system.

workout.cooldown: Roll gently onto your side, pause for a breath, then come to a comfortable seated position. Take 5 slow box breaths — inhale 4 counts, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4 — before standing.

product.name: TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller
product.blurb: Five minutes of rolling saves hours of stiffness. Non-negotiable on hard training days.

meal.title: Spiced Pork Tenderloin Bowl with Roasted Cauliflower and Green Tahini
ingredients: 1/2 cup cooked brown rice or farro
Small handful of fresh parsley or cilantro
meal.instructions: 1. Toss cauliflower in olive oil, salt, and pepper and roast at 220C/425F for 20 minutes until golden. 2. Season pork medallions with paprika, cumin, garlic powder, salt, and pepper. Sear in a hot pan with a little olive oil for 3-4 minutes per side until cooked through. 3. Blend or whisk tahini, lemon juice, fresh herbs, and a splash of water into a loose green dressing. 4. Layer rice or farro in a bowl, top with cauliflower and pork, and drizzle generously with green tahini.
meal.tip: Pound the pork medallions lightly before seasoning for faster, more even cooking — critical when you're racing the clock at dinnertime.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
